headings.text: From rivers and rigging to the abyssal plain.
Subtropical accumulation zones, ranked by debris density.
What washes up — the 8 most-counted items in beach surveys.
Resin breakdown of ocean-bound plastic, by mass.
Top 8 plastic-emitting rivers, by annual flux to ocean.

paragraphs.text:
Roughly 380 million tons of plastic is produced each year. Of that, an estimated
            8 Mt escapes containment and reaches the ocean — 80% via 1,000 high-discharge rivers,
            18% from coastal dumping and tourism, and 2% from fisheries, shipping, and aquaculture gear. Once afloat,
            currents sort the debris into five subtropical gyres before fragmentation and biofouling drag it down to
            the seafloor, where the deepest microplastic was recovered at 10,975 m in the
            Mariana Trench.

Without intervention, plastic flux into the ocean is projected to
            triple by 2040, reaching ~29 Mt/yr. The accumulated stock could exceed
            600 Mt — outweighing the biomass of all wild marine vertebrates combined.
Source: Meijer et al., Science Advances (2021). The top 1,000 rivers carry ~80% of all river-borne ocean plastic; the top 8 alone account for ~27%.
Six pledges, one progress chart, and a global protection timeline — see what works.
Peer-reviewed ocean health data, visualized for climate researchers and educators. Open data, open methodology, no paywalls.

Annual output
          380 Mt
          Virgin plastic resin produced globally in 2023.

Entering ocean
          8.0 Mt/yr
          ≈ 22,000 metric tons per day — one truckload every minute.

River-borne share
          80 %
          Routed via the top 1,000 polluting rivers, half from Asia.

Recycled fraction
          9 %
          Only one-eleventh of waste ever made has been recycled.

Single-use share
          40 %
          Packaging discarded within 12 months of manufacture.

N. Pacific
          1.6M km² · 79,000 t
          The Great Pacific Garbage Patch — twice the size of Texas; 1.8 trillion pieces.

N. Atlantic
          1.1M km² · 31,000 t
          Sargasso convergence; 200,000 pieces/km² near Bermuda.

Indian Ocean
          0.9M km² · 29,000 t
          Anchored between Madagascar and Australia; under-surveyed.

S. Pacific
          2.6M km² · 22,000 t
          Easter Island sector; lower density but vast extent.

S. Atlantic
          0.8M km² · 18,000 t
          Smallest gyre, dominated by Brazil & W. Africa coastal sources.
90% of seabirds now have plastic in their digestive tract.
Entanglement and gut blockage from ghost gear & macrolitter.
Mean surface concentration across the 5 gyres.
17% are IUCN-listed as near-threatened or worse.

Deep Current Ocean Data · v4.2 Atlas Ocean Health Species Atlas Temperature Rise Plastic Crisis Take Action Depth Scale 0m 200 1k 4k 6k 11k Atlas / Plastic Crisis / Lifecycle Flow Live · Updated 2024-Q4 Chapter 04 · Polymer Pollution The Plastic Crisis 8 million tons enter the ocean every year — tracing the river-to-seafloor lifecycle of marine plastic. Synthesis 2024 From rivers and rigging to the abyssal plain. Roughly 380 million tons of plastic is produced each year. Of that, an estimated 8 Mt escapes containment and reaches the ocean — 80% via 1,000 high-discharge rivers,
            18% from coastal dumping and tourism, and 2% from fisheries, shipping, and aquaculture gear. Once afloat,
            currents sort the debris into five subtropical gyres before fragmentation and biofouling drag it down to
            the seafloor, where the deepest microplastic was recovered at 10,975 m in the
            Mariana Trench. Sources UNEP 2023 Jambeck et al. 2021 OECD Global Plastics Outlook NOAA Marine Debris The Ocean Cleanup ⚠ Trajectory Without intervention, plastic flux into the ocean is projected to triple by 2040 , reaching ~29 Mt/yr. The accumulated stock could exceed 600 Mt — outweighing the biomass of all wild marine vertebrates combined. PROJ-REF · IPCC AR6 / Pew 2020 Lifecycle of Ocean Plastic Production Annual output 380 Mt Virgin plastic resin produced globally in 2023. Entering ocean 8.0 Mt/yr ≈ 22,000 metric tons per day — one truckload every minute. River-borne share 80 % Routed via the top 1,000 polluting rivers, half from Asia. Recycled fraction 9 % Only one-eleventh of waste ever made has been recycled. Single-use share 40 % Packaging discarded within 12 months of manufacture. Pacific 2.6M km² · 22,000 t Easter Island sector; lower density but vast extent. S. Atlantic 0.8M km² · 18,000 t Smallest gyre, dominated by Brazil & W. Africa coastal sources. Common Debris What washes up — the 8 most-counted items in beach surveys. 21% Bottles 17% Bags 12% Wrappers 11% Cigarettes 9% Straws 8% Cups 7% Fishing Net 15% Microplastic Polymer Composition Plastic Types · 2023 Resin breakdown of ocean-bound plastic, by mass. 7 polymer classes Composition of Marine Plastic Debris Stacked bar · % of total mass · n = 4.2M particles classified by FTIR spectroscopy 0% 20% 40% 60% 80% 100% PET 26% HDPE 18% PVC 10% LDPE 16% PP 21% PS 6% 3% ① ② ③ ④ ⑤ ⑥ ⑦ SOURCE: UNEP · OCEAN CLEANUP · 2023 GLOBAL SURVEY · FTIR-CLASSIFIED · n=4,210,338 Polymer ID codes per ASTM D7611. Density determines whether a fragment floats or sinks: PET, PVC, PS > seawater (1.025 g/cm³). [FIG · PLST-04] PET ① Bottles, fibers ρ 1.38 · sinks HDPE ② Caps, jugs ρ 0.95 · floats PVC ③ Pipes, film ρ 1.38 · sinks LDPE ④ Bags, wrap ρ 0.92 · floats PP ⑤ Caps, rope ρ 0.90 · floats PS ⑥ Foam, cutlery ρ 1.05 · sinks Other ⑦ Mixed, ABS variable River Discharge Rankings Top 8 plastic-emitting rivers, by annual flux to ocean. Rank River Basin Discharge (t/yr) % Global 01 Pasig Philippines 63,700 6.43 02 Tullahan Philippines 42,140 4.25 03 Ulhas India 38,150 3.85 04 Klang Malaysia 31,200 3.15 05 Meycauayan Philippines 29,100 2.93 06 Pampanga Philippines 26,800 2.71 07 Libmanan Philippines 22,500 2.27 08 Ganges India / Bangladesh 18,300 1.85 Source: Meijer et al., Science Advances (2021). The top 1,000 rivers carry ~80% of all river-borne ocean plastic; the top 8 alone account for ~27%. By 2050, the ocean is projected to contain more plastic than fish by mass — unless we cut single-use production at source. — Ellen MacArthur Foundation, The New Plastics Economy (revised 2024) Take measurable action. Six pledges, one progress chart, and a global protection timeline — see what works. Go to Action Hub Deep Current Peer-reviewed ocean health data, visualized for climate researchers and educators.
